Attached for consideration by the Executive Directors is a request from Chad for arrangements under the enhanced structural adjustment facility. This subject, together with the policy framework paper for Chad (to be issued shortly), is tentatively scheduled for discussion on Friday, September 1, 1995. A draft decision appears on page 16.
Mr. Riechel (ext. 34548),Mr. Tchatchouang (ext. 38428),or Mr. Cossé (ext. 3873l) is available to answer technical or factual questions relating to this paper prior to the Board discussion.

Unless the Documents Preparation Section (ext. 36760) is otherwise notified, the document will be transmitted, in accordance with the procedures approved by the Executive Board and with the appropriate deletions, to the African Development Bank (AfDB) and the European Commission (EC), following its consideration by the Executive Board.

I. Introduction

In the attached letter to the Managing Director dated August 8, 1995, the Government of Chad requests a three-year arrangement under the enhanced structural adjustment facility (ESAF) in an amount equivalent to SDR 49.56 million (l20 percent of quota), and the first annual arrangement thereunder, amounting to SDR 16.52 million (40 percent of quota). The requested arrangements are in support of Chad's macroeconomic and structural adjustment program set out in the medium-term policy framework paper (PFP), which was prepared in collaboration with the staffs of the Fund and the World Bank (to be issued). 1/ The policy measures for the period July l, 1995-June 30, 1996 are described in the attached memorandum on economic and financial policies--MEFP (Appendix II).

The Fund and the World Bank staffs have worked together closely on Chad. 2/ The World Bank Group is supporting Chad's adjustment effort with an Economic and Financial Management Project (GEF) for $\mathtt{U S S10}$ million, which focuses on public institution building and reform, and technical assistance. It is also preparing two structural adjustment credits (SAC I and II) of $\mathtt{U S S40}$ million each, the first of which is expected to be approved later in 1995.

At the end of June 1995, Chad's outstanding use of Fund resources amounted to SDR 26.9 million, equivalent to 65.0 percent of quota. Assuming full disbursement of the loans under the proposed three-year ESAF arrangement, and taking into account repayments and repurchases due during the three-year arrangement period on previous SAF loans and purchases under the 1994 stand-by arrangement, Chad's financial obligations to the Fund would amount to 139.7 percent of quota at end-June l998 (Appendix I, Table 1).

Chad continues to avail itself of the transitional arrangements of Article XIV. However, as a member of the Central African Economic and Monetary Community, it maintains an exchange system that is free of restrictions on the making of payments and transfers for current international transactions. The l994 Article IV consultation was concluded on February 17, 1995.

The quality, coverage, and timeliness of statistical information in Chad remains poor and will be the subject of technical assistance over the period foreseen for the ESAF arrangement. Statistical issues in Chad have been discussed most recently in SM/95/2l, Appendix IV.

After several years during which the dialogue between Chad and the Fund and World Bank was constrained by domestic political instability, operational support to Chad was re-established in connection with the economic adjustment program that was adopted in the aftermath of the devaluation of the CFA franc in January l994. The principal objective of the program was to ensure that the price and incentive effects of the exchange rate action would be accompanied by appropriate measures to achieve a lasting improvement in competitiveness and growth (Appendix I, Table 2). In support of the program, the Fund approved on March 23, 1994 a l2-month stand-by arrangement (SBA) in an amount equivalent to SDR l6.52 million (40 percent of quota), and the IDA approved an Economic Recovery Credit of ${\tt U S}\$20$ million.

Implementation of the $1994$ program encountered problems shortly after its inception and only the first purchase under the SBA was made. Strikes of civil servants calling for the resumption of salary payments and settlement of salary arrears crippled the administration and halted revenue collection. Transit problems in northern Cameroon sharply reduced imports, thereby curtailing customs revenue and fueling inflation. These problems were exacerbated by an insufficient initial commitment to reforms, weaknesses in administration, and shortcomings in economic policy implementation. As a result, several performance criteria for end-June 1994, including notably those on revenue collection, were missed (Appendix I, Table 3). To bring policies back on track and establish a record of policy implementation, a staff-monitored program (SMP) was developed for the period October $1994$ -June l995. The program sought to strengthen revenue performance and to broaden and deepen the structural adjustment effort, including through the implementation of an extensive FAD-supported program of indirect tax and customs tariff measures consistent with the reforms agreed at the regional level within the framework of the Central African's Economic and Customs Union (UDEAC). l/

Overall, the economy has responded positively to the improvement in competitiveness that followed the devaluation and, more recently, to the firming up of the policy environment associated with the program (Appendix I, Table 2). Performance was also helped by a more propitious external environment (increase in cotton prices) and satisfactory rainfalls. In the event, five years of economic stagnation have given way to real GDP growth of some 5 1/4 percent in l994, and a similar outcome is expected during 1995-96; after an initial surge, inflation has been declining sharply since mid-1994, and the external current account deficit (excluding official transfers) has been cut by almost 5 percentage points of GDP to
$223/4$ percent of GDP. l/ At the same time, strengthened commitment and improved management of economic policy have resulted in a much improved design and implementation of economic reforms.

The results of this turnaround in commitment, economic policies, and external conditions have been reflected in Chad's encouraging performance from mid-1994 under the staff-monitored program (Appendix I, Table 3). The strong improvement in revenue collection has reversed the deterioration in the current primary deficit of the public finances, which would have reached 9 percent of GDP on the basis of revenue performance during the first half of the year, instead of the 6 percent ultimately recorded. The increase in revenue has also allowed for the resumption of critical government spending and permitted the partial settlement of payments arrears to domestic suppliers and civil servants. Similarly, full implementation of the structural measures called for by the staff-monitored program has advanced fiscal reform, supported competitiveness, and reduced the need for subsidization of public enterprises. In recognition of the progress made since mid-1994, Chad was in February l995 granted by its Paris Club creditors the rescheduling of its eligible stock of payments arrears at end-March 1994 and debt service payments for the period April 1994-March 1995. 2/

1/ Balance of payments developments, foreign reserves, and monetary growth in l994/95 were affected by a number of exceptional factors. In the first nine months of $1994$ , the constraints on imports mentioned earlier, combined with the effects of the devaluation on export earnings, inward transfers and capital repatriation, and the inflow of foreign program assistance boosted Chad's contribution to the foreign reserve position of the BEAC, the regional Central Bank, from CFAF 8.5 billion in December 1993 (adjusted) to CFAF 46 billion in September l994, and raised year-on-year money growth for September to 75 percent. This trend was reversed in late 1994 and early l995 by a strong increase in imports, an unusually large cyclical lag between payments for fertilizer imports and the repatriation of cotton export earnings, and increasing transfers abroad, including by Nigerian residents. All these factors resulted in a sharp and sudden drop in both money and net foreign assets. The magnitude of these recorded movements, however, may be exaggerated by the increase in the stock of unsorted currency notes (billets non-triés) at the Central Bank, which implied an underrecording of both currency in circulation and net foreign assets of Chad to the extent that this stock included currency notes of other BEAC member countries. The tables on monetary developments attempt to correct for these technical factors from l994 onward.

2/ Debt relief was granted through a terms of reference flow rescheduling, on Naples terms, with a 67 percent reduction in net present value terms of the consolidated obligations (excluding previously rescheduled debt service).
1. Overview

The authorities emphasized that they viewed economic reform as an integral element of Chad's move toward a pluralistic form of government. l/ Particular attention would hence be given to the development of private initiative and a further liberalization of the economy. In this context, the conclusion of a medium-term economic adjustment program that could be supported by the Bretton Woods institutions was accorded a high priority. At the same time, the authorities pointed to the severe constraints--physical and financial--that they were facing. Already one of the poorest countries in the world, Chad had suffered from recurrent internal conflicts and a growing erosion of donor confidence and support. The related weaknesses in general administration and economic management had been evident in the early stages of the l994 program. Since then, considerable progress had been made because the formulation of a coherent economic program, coupled with a resumption of adjustment financing, had permitted them to relaunch the economy and invigorate public administration. To enhance confidence at home and abroad in the authorities' commitment to sound economic management, it was important to sustain the momentum of adjustment and performance that had emerged. To that end, they were determined to take strong, broad-based, and front-loaded action. At the same time, they pointed to the need for appropriate external financing and technical assistance in support of the program.
2. Adjustment strategy and policy objectives

Against this background, program discussions, as detailed in the PFP and in the accompanying MEFP, focused on: (i) maintaining macroeconomic stability to foster private sector development, safeguard external competitiveness, strengthen the saving-investment balance, and protect the vulnerable groups in society from high and variable inflation; (ii) improving the functioning of public administration, with emphasis on the efficiency and transparency of public sector financial management; (iii) reinforcing the revenue departments, to provide for adequate domestic resource mobilization; (iv) revitalizing the private sector and strengthening the response mechanisms of the economy through privatization, price and trade liberalization, easing of market access, and a streamlining of the regulatory environment; and (v) defining clear spending priorities, with a view to raising investment in human and physical capital. The discussions also sought to identify .areas where the design and implementation of economic reforms might benefit from technical assistance and training (Appendix I, Table 4), as well as areas in which preparatory work could usefully be initiated for future structural reforms.

The authorities were confident that the simultaneous pursuit of macroeconomic stability and structural reform would result in a significant improvement in economic performance, provided that the security situation could be kept stable and social peace preserved. Continued and visible improvement in economic conditions was therefore essential. Their objective for the period l995-98 was to achieve an average growth rate of real GDP of 5 percent, so that, after many years of decline, per capita income could grow again. l/ Prospects for such gains would undoubtedly further strengthen supply incentives, and thereby contribute to the reduction in inflation to the target range of 3-5 percent. Inflation control would also benefit from trade liberalization and a more general opening up of markets, not least because these measures would tend to cushion the impact of planned price liberalization.

Macroeconomic stabilization is to be pursued through a reduction of public and private dissaving. Over the program period, the share of consumption in GDP is to be brought down to roughly the level of GDP compared with an average share in GDP of more than ll0 percent during 1992-93 (Appendix I, Table 5). 2/ Despite pressing spending needs, the program calls for public sector domestic dissaving to fall by some 6 percentage points, to less than 1 percent of GDP in l998, thereby about offsetting a prospective parallel decline in exceptional external financing. Private sector domestic dissaving is also to decline, albeit by less than in the public sector because of the relative incompressibility of per capita consumption, one of the lowest in the world, and because the programmed increase in the tax/GDP ratio and the prospective deterioration in the terms of trade will curtail the scope for a rapid improvement in saving generation. Nevertheless, as confidence in the domestic economy rises and investment opportunities increase in the country, net private transfers are expected to turn around and boost private sector financial resources and investment. Together with the expected increase in public investment, this development is projected substantially to increase the country's medium-term growth potential and employment opportunities. Although Chad's dependency on foreign savings would remain high at the end of the program period in

1998, virtually all of such savings should be in support of investment. Owing to the usual lags between an upturn in investment and a strengthening of the external balance, the narrowing of the external current account deficit will remain modest over the program period. However, as the production base is durably strengthened, positive private and public sector saving and a more substantial improvement in the external balances should emerge, and this will be reinforced by the coming on stream of oil production toward the turn of the century. l/
3. Economic policies for 1995-96
a. Economic and financial management

Successful formulation and implementation of economic policies require further improvements in the functioning of public administration, the budget process, and financial management. The existing system of economic policy committees has served the authorities well, but its effectiveness can be further enhanced. 2/ In particular, the flow and transparency of information to policymakers, donors, the business community, and the general public, while improved substantially since mid-l994, still needs to be broadened and made more timely and coherent. Measures to that effect have already been taken, as witnessed by the increasingly comprehensive committee reports. Related improvements in economic management at the policy making and supervisory levels has to be accompanied by a more general strengthening of implementation capacity. This will require a comprehensive reform of the civil service, an objective that will be pursued with the assistance of the World Bank, starting later in l995. Special emphasis should be placed on strengthening the incentive system and identifying needed skills and appropriate training.
b. Fiscal policies

Fiscal policy aims to reduce the current primary deficit from 6 percent of GDP in 1994 to $21/4$ percent in 1995 and 1 1/4 percent in l996 (Appendix I, Table 6). Achievement of these objectives, as well as the advancement of fiscal reform and restructuring of government spending, will require a significant improvement in budget preparation, execution, and monitoring. A study on these issues is under preparation with the assistance of the World Bank, and its recommendations are to be implemented from l996 onward.

Regarding the fiscal deficit objectives, the authorities felt that, while there was scope for savings in selected areas, a general expenditure reduction was not appropriate, given the urgency of spending in priority areas and the poor state of equipment and supplies. The situation thus puts a premium on improvements in revenue collection, a strategy that has already been pursued with some success since mid-l994. At the same time, however, some of the recent acceleration in tax collection has been due to one-off factors, which can not be projected into the future. Moreover, it has become clear that there are limits to the speed with which revenue collection can be improved and reforms can become effective. First, especially in the current setting in Chad, the reorganization of, and improvements in coordination among, the revenue departments require time, as does the adaptation of work practices and documentation to the new tax and customs laws. The authorities also noted that taxpayers' compliance with changes aimed at strengthening revenue collection either through increase in tax rates or a broadening of the tax base, was often weak initially. This behavior could severely hamper revenue collection in the early stages of tax reform, as had become evident in early 1995 in connection with the implementation of the turnover tax (TCA) and excise taxes on domestic transactions. Nevertheless, the authorities reiterated their determination to increase government receipts substantially so that the revenue-to-GDP ratio would rise to from 7 1/2 percent in l994 to 9 percent in l995 and about $93/4$ percent in 1996.

To achieve their revenue objectives, the authorities will continue to assign priority to the effective and timely implementation of the UDEAC tax and customs reforms, not least because these reforms will simplify the tax system and broaden the tax base, while harmonizing taxation in the region. A new rate structure for customs duties, a turnover tax with only two rates (5 and l5 percent), and a number of excise taxes (at rates between 5 and 30 percent) have already been introduced in line with UDEAC agreements and are estimated to account for about l percentage point of the increase in tax revenue in l995. Measures for l996 will concentrate on a further broadening of the tax base. This will include in particular the renegotiation, before end-l995, of the special enterprise conventions and adjustments in the Investment Code to eliminate related tax exemptions and make them compatible with the new tax laws. Moreover, a single taxpayer code and file has been introduced to improve the monitoring and collection of tax liabilities. In addition, the authorities will continue to rigorously implement the extensive and ambitious action plan that has been developed for the customs office with the assistance of a FAD technical assistance mission and resident expert. Principal actions in the second half of l995 and in 1996 will consist of a further strengthening of customs monitoring and control, a widening of the assessment of customs duties on the basis of updated c.i.f. values, and the expansion of training programs and use of computerized information. Since the plan was launched in September $1994$ , the functioning of the customs office has improved measurably, and it is likely that in 1995 customs revenue will almost double compared with $1994$ , and continue to rise strongly in l996 and beyond. In contrast, the collection of customs duties on petroleum products, which has been entrusted to a private preinspection company, has remained highly disappointing for reasons not yet completely

understood. In collaboration with the World Bank, the authorities are currently undertaking a special review the matter. At the latest by end-1995, they will formulate an action program that will focus on measures to effectively control fraud, and that should permit an increase in revenue from this source by about 50 percent in both 1996.

On the expenditure side, the program objectives are to bring current spending down from $151/4$ percent of GDP in l994 to $131/4$ percent in both 1995 and l996, while shifting priorities toward investment in human and physical capital and the strengthening of revenue departments. Accordingly. the wage bill will be kept constant in real terms despite an increase in staff in priority areas, as well as pressure for increases in civil service wages and a full payment of wage arrears. To meet the salary objective for 1995, the authorities have essentially offset the financial effects of the 10 percent increase in wage rates (effective January l, 1995) by implementing comprehensive cuts in allowances and other benefits. Regarding government employment, the scope for further reductions in staff has narrowed after last year's large-scale dismissal of staff above retirement age (sone 1,000 persons). Retirement rules will continue to be enforced strictly and other possibilities for reductions in the government payroll will be explored in the context of the civil service census that is now under way with World Bank assistance and will be completed by end-November 1995. Together, these measure should permit a further reduction in the Government payroll by some 500 persons. Potentially significant budgetary savings on wages can also be expected from a recently introduced program to make up fol salary arrears by handing over public land titles to civil servants. The initiative has met with considerable success and will be pursued in the future because it not only reduces government payments obligations but also increases private land ownership.

Noting the recent slowdown in the demobilization program, the authorities reiterated their determination to reduce the size of the army to no more than 25,000 men. Progress in the execution of the program has encountered three main obstacles: first, a government initiative to reduce the retirement age and maximum years of service has failed to obtain the support of the transitional national assembly; second, in the interest of ensuring a peaceful climate for the upcoming elections, an agreement has been concluded between the Government and a rebel group for the integration of the group's members into the regular forces, raising their size by some 1,500 to 32,000; finally, residual foreign financing available for demobilization is insufficient to reach the agreed objective because it will in the best of circumstances cover departure allowances for no more than 4,000 men. As budgetary resources for a financing of demobilization are not available, the authorities will make a special effort to secure additional external financial assistance from the donor community in order to achieve the objective of 25,000 men by end-June l996.

The composition of government spending is to be shifted in favor of investment in human capital. In particular, for both l995 and l996, a 20 percent expansion in budgetary allowances (in real terms) is planned for primary education and basic health services. Moreover, measures will be

taken to achieve a satisfactory level of supplies of essential generic drugs. Expenditure increases in these areas will be compensated in part by a reduction in subsidies of approximately CFAF 750 million, made possible by the increases in electricity prices and the liberalization of sugar prices. Regarding capital spending, a complete and well-documented public investment program has recently been presented for the first time. It will permit a better rationalization of investment activities of government and donors alike. In the current situation of tight public finances, budget-financed investment is to be limited to the local counterpart funds for foreignfinanced projects.
C Monetary and financial sector policies

Monetary management in Chad will be guided by the reform measures at the regional BEAC level, which seek to increase reliance on market mechanisms and the use of indirect instruments of monetary control. Given the monetary arrangements in the BEAC zone, monetary policy will aim at protecting Chad's contribution to the foreign reserve position of the BEAC. Given the recent sharp weakening of BEAC's overall position in the operations account has indeed heightened the authorities' concern on this score. Accordingly, despite the further small decline in the velocity of money that is expected, the program calls for a prudent approach toward domestic credit expansion.

In the short run, given the dominance of the cotton sector in domestic credit demand and the favorable earnings outlook for COTONTCHAD, as well as the programmed settlement of payments arrears to suppliers, Chad is unlikely to face a high demand for credit, and the program provides for a decline in aggregate private sector credit from CFAF 3l.3 billion at end- $1994$ to CFAF 29.3 billion at end-l996. This development would nonetheless accommodate a satisfactory development of credit to the rest of the economy, excluding COTONTCHAD (Appendix I, Tables 7 and 8). With net credit to the Government increasing by no more than the counterpart of net disbursements from the Fund under the ESAF-supported program, it would also allow for a further strengthening of the banking system's overall net foreign reserves position. To ensure that this improvement strengthens official reserves, the BEAC intends to make active use as of early-l996 of the reverse auction mechanism, whose introduction was decided at the BEAC's July 1995 Board meeting. Chad's banking system is in a relatively sound situation, and the authorities are determined to keep it so in the future. They will therefore continue to lend their full support to the regional banking commission (COBAC) in its efforts to assure that prudential ratios and related dispositions are fully adhered to.
4. Structural reforms

The principal objectives of structural reform are the broadening and revitalization of the private sector. Main actions to that effect are (a) an acceleration of the privatization program, and measures to strengthen the operations of public enterprises; (b) the removal of minimum wage
regulations; (c) the abolishment of import and export licenses; and (d) a comprehensive reform of exchange regulations.

Under the plan to accelerate the privatization program, the authorities, in collaboration with the World Bank, have identified nine public enterprises (mostly in the tourism, insurance, and manufacturing sectors) to be put up for sale before the end of l995. In addition, new initiatives for a strengthening of competitiveness and profitability will be introduced regarding state enterprises in the cotton and sugar sectors and the public utility.

As a result of the devaluation and the prudent wage policy that has been pursued since then, profitability has been restored in the cotton sector. Priority is now to be given to raising production to a level that corresponds to the comparative advantage that Chad possesses in cotton production and that fully exploits the scope for economies of scale. To this end, appropriate adjustments in producer prices (including a premium when appropriate) and necessary increases in investments in transportation and ginning capacities will be decided annually in the context of a mediumterm strategy in collaboration with the shareholders of COToNTCHAD, as well as the World Bank. Progress is also to be made by end-l995 on the financial restructuring of CoTONTCHAD, including the elimination of past loss carryovers and the settling of cross-debts between COTONTCHAD and the Government. l/ To balance these objectives, and to be able to respond appropriately to the instability of weather conditions and world cotton prices, the authorities have opted for retaining flexibility in the profitsharing mechanism and the setting of producer prices.

In the sugar sector, the past system of state intervention that combined price and profit margin controls with restrictions on imports has failed, and the sector is to be liberalized. The temporary surtax on sugar imports has already been lifted and price and profit margin controls on sugar have been removed to strengthen competition at the wholesale and retail levels and achieve a more effective control over fraud. To the same end, and to rein in price hikes and fraud, the authorities will henceforth stop setting annual import quotas and allow sugar imports to respond more flexibly to excess demand in domestic markets. In this connection, they noted that even though competitiveness relative to most regional sugar producers was established, the local sugar manufacturer (SONASUT) could not compete with, and needed protection from, imports sold at dumping prices.

Nevertheless, to further improve competitiveness and contain fraudulent imports of sugar, the authorities and the French shareholders plan a significant expansion of production to more economical, lower cost levels.

In the public utility (STEE), substantial cost cuts and improvements in productivity have already been achieved. Further efforts will center on raising the recovery ratio for invoices. A possible transfer of management to private contractors is also under discussion with the World Bank. Moreover, STEE losses will be curtailed by the l0 percent increase in electricity prices that became effective July l, 1995. However, given the STEE's dependence on imported oil and the fact that Chad is a landlocked country with high transportation costs, the long-term solution to the utility's financial problems will depend primarily on the speed at which national oil reserves can be brought on stream.

Labor market flexibility is to be enhanced through a comprehensive revision of the Labor Code. In particular, with a view to allowing the social partners to make discretionary decisions on the balancing of potential trade-offs between wage levels and employment, the existing minimum wage legislation is to be rescinded in favor of freely negotiated agreements between employers and employees. The new Labor Code will also grant freedom to the contracting parties to decide whether agreements should be reached at the national or sectoral level.

With a view to easing administration, eliminating the complexity and arbitrariness of the old import and export control system, stimulating trade and commerce, and cutting a costly burden on enterprises, the authorities have decided to remove the current system of import and export licenses. Needed monitoring and control over imports for health and security reasons will henceforth be assigned to the customs office on the basis of a restricted negative list. Moreover, the authorities intend to reduce fraud, achieve a better assessment of customs values, and improve the verification of foreign exchange transactions by establishing efficient channels of communication on trade and payments operations between the customs office, the Treasury, the Ministry of Commerce, and the Central Bank. Finally, to remove uncertainties and the risks of arbitrary decisions, the authorities will replace the outdated national guidelines for foreign exchange transactions by the exchange regulations proposed for the BEAC area as a whole in September l993. In this context, the authorities confirmed their intention to maintain a liberal trade and payments system and support a timely move to Article VIII status in the Fund.

Improving the quality, coverage, and timeliness of statistical information is an important concern of the authorities. Given the current shortage of physical and financial resources, priority is initially to be given to the generation of critical operational data--on government financial operations, and on monetary, balance of payments, and price and trade developments--that are needed for the effective implementation and monitoring of the adjustment program. Data collection, processing, and dissemination by the Technical Committee is therefore to be improved and expanded, while all government departments and agencies feeding information to the Committee have been requested to do so in a thorough and timely manner. At a later stage, the coverage of data collection will be broadened and deepened in line with additional reporting needs to be identified with the help of Fund assistance.
IV. External Sector, External Financing, and Capacity to Repay the Fund

External prospects are expected to improve progressively with the increase in supply incentives and the change in the composition and level of import demand, where the latter is expected to reflect in particular the increase in foreign-financed public investments and upturn in private investment. The full effects of the structural improvement in the external current account are consequently projected to occur with a lag (Appendix I, Table 9). l/ There are a number of risks to the program's fiscal and external balances: slippages in wage policy that could entail demand pressures and reduce competitiveness; election-related increases in government spending, to the extent that they are not foreign financed; the recurrence of unfavorable weather conditions; and adverse movements in the terms of trade. For example, a decline in international cotton prices to the average level prevailing during the period 1992-93, which ceteris paribus, implies a 25 percent drop in prices, would raise the current account deficit by $2\ 1/4$ percentage points of GDP, slow the pace of reserve accumulation, and raise the debt service ratio in l998 by about one percentage point to 12 percent (Appendix I, Table l0). Similarly, a l0 percent increase in international manufactured goods prices as of l996 would raise the current account deficit by 1 1/2 percentage points of GDP. 2/

Chad's external financing requirements and the sources of exceptional assistance are discussed in the accompanying policy framework paper. For 1995 and 1996, the financing gaps that remain after disbursements by the Fund and the World Bank amount to CFAF 32.9 billion and CFAF l7.2 billion, respectively. Reasonable assurances exist that it can be covered by (i) bilateral aid of CFAF 28.8 billion in 1995, and CFAF l6.1 billion in 1996 (mostly from France, the European Union, and the United States); (ii) the rescheduling of eligible debt service by Paris Club and other bilateral creditors of an additional CFAF 0.5 billion in l995, and CFAF l.1 billion in 1996; and (iii) the deferral, in l995, of some CFAF 3.6 billion of payments on the postal debt, for which a request has been made. Adjusters for shortfalls or increases in external financing would be as follows: shortfalls before end-l995 would result in a temporar: postponement of the programmed elimination of external payments arrears,

which is envisaged by end-l995 in the current financial programming. Shortfalls that persist during the period January-June l996 would result in a reduction in the planned settlement of domestic payments arrears up to the total amount foreseen for l996 as a whole (CFAF 8.6 billion). For larger shortfalls, a consultation with the Fund staff would ensue. On the other hand, domestic or foreign financing larger than programmed would be used in equal parts for an expansion in the settlement of domestic payments arrears and reduction in the net credit to the Government.

Independently of the large increases in fiscal revenues and foreign reserves that are expected to accrue from future exploitation of national oil reserves, Chad should experience a strengthening in the viability of its external position, the sustainability of its debt burden, and its capacity to repay the Fund as a result of the projected improvement in the fiscal position, and an increase in domestic saving more generally. The country's prospects will also improve owing to the expansion of its growth potential in response to the upturn of investment in infrastructure and machinery and equipment. On the basis of the proposed schedule of disbursements (Appendix I, Table ll), the debt service ratio to the Fund is projected to peak in 1997 at 4.7 percent of exports of goods and nonfactor services, drop to 0.5 percent by 2000, and then to average around 2 percent over 2001-05 (Appendix I, Tables 12 and l3). The overall debt service ratio will also peak in l997 at l6.l percent and then display a pattern similar to that of the debt service ratio to the Fund, dropping to below l3 percent by 2005. Chad's ability to remain current on its financial obligations to the Fund should be further enhanced by technical assistance on debt management to be received from the World Bank. The staff will also seek to enhance it by recommending the acquisition ahead of time of the SDRs needed to meet Fund charges and repayments. Under these assumptions, Chad should be able to meet its payments obligations to the Fund on a timely basis.
V. Prior Actions and Program Monitoring

All policy measures listed in paragraph 26 of the attached MEFP constituted prior actions and have already been implemented by the Chadian authorities. Performance under the first-year ESAF arrangement will be monitored on the basis of the financial and structural performance criteria and benchmarks presented in paragraph 27 of the MEFP. Benchmarks will apply to end-September l995 and performance criteria to end-December 1995. The second disbursement will be conditional upon observance of the performance criteria at end-December l995 and completion of the midterm review, which will take place no later than March 15, l996. The review will reassess the indicative benchmarks for end-March and end-June l996 and will pay particular attention to the performance of the revenue departments and focus on ways that could further increase tax revenue in l996 and beyond. It will also assess progress in the currentness of debt service payments and on the reduction in payments arrears to suppliers.

Starting from a very difficult situation and serious initial slippages under the original l994 program, the Chadian authorities have since mid-1994 made much progress in the design and implementation of corrective economic and structural adjustment policies. Internalization of program objectives has improved, efforts to raise the level of acceptance of policies through an open dialogue and increased transparency are yielding encouraging results, and the responsiveness and efficiency of public administration are increasing. It is now important to strengthen this momentum of reform and expand its reach. The authorities' assurances that their economic program is an integral and critical part of the broader political transition toward a pluralistic form of goverrment are important for its credibility and acceptance. This should also_ protect program execution against risks of derailment during the period leading up to the election in early 1996.

Chad nonetheless has a long way to go if its objective of significantly raising the standard of living of the population is to be achieved on a lasting basis. Further progress in the establishment of credibility and confidence at home and abroad will require that program implementation be predictable and unwavering at both the policy making and administrative levels. First, the work of the economic policy committees must be further streamlined and expanded. Second, the planned civil service reform must aim at a general increase in efficiency in public administration. It should establish a clearer set of priorities and an in-depth profile of required skills. It should also set strong signals for a performance-oriented advancement system for civil servants. At the same time, the budget process needs to be strengthened and rationalized and technical assistance should be sought to help in this effort.

Despite pressing spending needs, fiscal consolidation must be pursued with determination not only to reassure the public and donors of the authorities' resolve and capacity to achieve on a regular basis an orderly budget execution, but also to increase resources for public and private investment, assist in re-establishing macroeconomic balance, and support private sector development.

Fiscal action must first and foremost center on further improving revenue performance. Measures to that effect need to include above all (i) the full implementation of the UDEAC tax reform; (ii) a better coordination and cooperation among revenue departments; (iii) the effective use of the single taxpayer code and file; (iv) implementation, at the customs office, of the extensive action plan--developed with the help of the FAD technical assistance--to broaden the tax base for import duties, shorten payments lags, and reduce fraud; and (v) the early resolution of the problem of customs duty collection on petroleum products.  The revenue objective for 1996 should be considered a minimum, and every effort should be made to surpass it, including through additional revenue actions.

On the expenditure side, budget constraints impose strict prioritization while the growth orientation of the program requires that spending favor investment in human and physical capital. The wage bill thus must be kept tightly in check. Moreover, at least in l995, there is no scope for a reduction in wage arrears, other than through compensation in kind. The civil service census must be given high priority to eliminate ghost employment and facilitate control of the payroll and wage bill. Regarding military spending, all possible avenues should be explored, and energetic action taken, to speed up demobilization.

In the monetary policy area, the authorities should lend their full support to the implementation of reforms at the regional level that broaden the use indirect instruments of monetary control and increase reliance on market mechanisms. Similarly, in the relevant regional bodies, they should press for the early adoption of Article VIII status by the BEAC member states.

The strengthening of structural policies should be pursued with energy, and should be based on a further liberalization of the economy and an unwavering commitment to private sector-led growth. In this context, the staff attaches considerable importance to the planned abolition of government-determined minimum wages and freeing of the wage formation process, which constitute an important step toward greater labor market flexibility and better factor allocation. Resource allocation will also benefit from the continuation of the price liberalization_ program under which the authorities have recently freed an additional number of prices. Moreover, the removal of import and export licenses is critical because it will remove a considerable administrative and financial burden from enterprises and make a major contribution to the strengthening of commerce and trade. Finally, private sector activity and planning should be eased by the intended harmonization of exchange regulations, which should be expedited.

The divestiture program needs to be given increased impetus, and where applicable in close cooperation with other shareholders. In the critical cotton sector, priority should in the current setting be given to a further strengthening of long-term competitiveness by giving appropriate rewards to producers and assuring the financing of investment objectives that seek to expand capacities to level that correspond with Chad's comparative advantage. Similarly, in the sugar sector, the liberalization of prices and imports can be expected to encourage efficiency and help control fraud. At the same time, competitiveness should be strengthened further by an expansion of production to more economical levels. Finally, significant progress needs to be made in the quality, coverage, and timeliness of statistical information. The focus on data that have an immediate operational use seems appropriate under current circumstances. For future reforms in this area, financial and technical assistance should be actively sought.

Given the strength of the proposed adjustment program, the authorities' demonstrated determination to implement strong policy measures under the SMP, and the projected favorable evolution of Chad's debt indicators and capacity to repay the Fund, the staff recommends approval of the requested arrangements under the enhanced structural adjustment facility.
In view of the foregoing, the following draft decision is proposed for adoption by the Executive Board:
1． The Government of Chad has requested a three-year arrangement under the enhanced structural adjustment facility, and the first annual arrangement thereunder.
2. The Fund notes the policy framework paper for Chad set forth in EBD/95/
