Attached hereto is a letter dated August 8, l995, with an annexed memorandum on the economic and financial policies of Chad, from the Minister of Finance and Computer Services, and the Minister of Planning and Cooperation of the Republic of Chad, requesting from the International Monetary Fund a three-year arrangement under the enhanced structural adjustment facility, and the first annual arrangement thereunder, and setting forth:

(i) the objectives and policies of the program to be supported by the three-year arrangement; and   
(ii) the objectives and policies of the program to be supported by the first annual arrangement.

To support these objectives and policies, the Fund grants the requested arrangements in accordance with the following provisions, and subject to the Instrument to Establish the Enhanced Structural Adjustment Facility Trust:

1.(a) For a period of three years from September ,1995, Chad will have the right to obtain loans from the Fund under the enhanced structural adjustment facility, in a total amount equivalent to SDR 49.56 million, which shall be provided from the Enhanced Structural Adjustment Facility Trust.
(b) The amount of each of the three annual arrangements will be iivalent of SDR 16.52 million.
(c) Under the first annual arrangement:

(i) the first loan, in an amount equivalent to SDR 8.26 million will be available on September l5, 1995 at the request of the Government of Chad; and   
(ii) the second loan, in an amount equivalent to SDR 8.26 million will be available on March 15, 1996 at the request of the Government of Chad subject to paragraph 2 below.
2.  Chad will not request disbursement of the second loan specified in paragraph l(c)(ii) above:
(a) if the Managing Director finds that the data at the end of December 1995 indicate that

(i) the limit on the net domestic assets of the Central Bank (BEAC)，or (ii) the limits on the net credit of banking system to the Government, or   
(iii) the floor for the minimum collection of domestic tax revenue, or (iv) the limit on the wage bill of the Central Government, or (v) the target for the reduction in domestic payments arrears, or (vi) the target for the reduction in external payments arrears, or   
(vii) the limits on the contracting or guaranteeing by the Government of new nonconcessional external debt, or   
viii) the limit on the contracting or guaranteeing by the Government of short-term external debt, or (ix) any of the two structural performance criteria set forth in paragraph 27 and Table l of the annexed memorandum was not observed; or
(b) if Chad

(i) has imposed or intensified restrictions on payments and transfers for current international transactions, or   
(ii) has introduced or modified multiple currency practices, or   
(iii) has concluded bilateral payments agreements which are inconsistent with Article VIII, or   
(iv) has imposed or intensified import restrictions for balance of payments reason; or (v) has incurred any new external payments arrears; or

(c) until the Fund determines that the midterm review of Chad's program, referred to in paragraph 5 of the attached letter dated August 8, 1995 and in paragraph 28 of the annexed memorandum has been completed.

If the Managing Director finds that any of the performance clauses that have been established in or under this paragraph 2 have not been met, the second loan specified in paragraph l(c)(ii) above may be made available only after consultation has taken place between the Fund and Chad, and understandings have been reached regarding the circumstances in which Chad may request the disbursement of that second loan.

3. Before approving the second annual arrangement, the Fund will appraise the progress of Chad in implementing the policies and reaching the objectives of the program supported by the first annual arrangement, taking into account primarily:

4. In accordance with paragraph 4 of the attached letter dated August 8, 1995, Chad will provide the Fund with such information as the Fund requests in connection with the progress of Chad in implementing the policies and reaching the objectives supported by these arrangements.

5. In accordance with paragraph 4 of the attached letter dated August 8, l995, during the period of the first annual arrangement, Chad will consult with the Managing Director on the adoption of any measures that may be appropriate at the initiative of the Government or whenever the Managing Director requests such a consultation. Moreover, after the period of the first annual arrangement, and while Chad has outstanding financial obligations to the Fund arising from loans under that arrangement, Chad will consult with the Fund from time to time, at the initiative of the Government or whenever the Managing Director requests consultation on Chad's economic and financial policies. These consultations may include correspondence and visits of officials of the Fund to Chad or representatives of Chad to the Fund.
Mr. Michel Camdessus Managing Director International Monetary Fund 700 19th Street, N.W. Washington, D.C. 20431
Dear Mr. Camdessus:

1. It is with satisfaction that we transmit to you the attached Memorandum on Economic and Financial Policies. The document sets out the range of macroeconomic and structural policy measures that Chad is committed to take during the period July 1, 1995 to June 30, 1996. The policy program constituted by these measures forms an integral part of a broader economic strategy, the orientations of which are described in the Policy Framework Paper for l995-98, which will be sent to you under separate cover. The two documents bear witness to Chad's commitment to economic reform under trying circumstances. The Government will spare no effort to build a national consensus around this program and implement its measures in a timely manner. In support of their program, the Government of Chad requests a three-year arrangement under the enhanced structural adjustment facility (ESAF) from the International Monetary Fund, for a total amount equivalent to SDR 49.56 million (l20 percent of quota), with access under the first annual arrangement thereunder in the amount equivalent to SDR l6.52 million (40 percent of quota).

2. Years of internal and external conflict have severely weakened much of Chad's economic, social, and administrative infrastructure. They have also undermined confidence at home and abroad and thwarted the emergence of a stable and peaceful climate for economic development. As part of their move toward pluralistic government, it is therefore the authorities' intention to set clear and unmistakable signals for their medium-term economic strategy, and to implement policies in a transparent and predictable fashion. Priority will be given to a strengthening of private sector initiative and activity, a rehabilitation of public administration, and a determined increase in investment in human capital. These objectives will be pursued in an environment of macroeconomic stability, the conditions for which will be created above all by decisive actions on the control of the public finances, and appropriate monetary and exchange rate policies in the context of regional arrangements.

3. The financing of the program for the first year is covered by anticipated assistance from bilateral and multilateral donors and creditors, as well as the rescheduling operations that have been requested by the Government of Chad from official bilateral creditors. The situation with regard to financing will be examined at the time of midterm review under the first annual arrangement.

4.  The Government of Chad believes that the policies and measures described in the attached Memorandum on Economic and Financial Policies for 1995-96 are adequate to achieve the objectives of the program, but will take any additional measures that may become 'necessary for this purpose. The Government will consult with the Fund on the adoption of any measures that may be needed, in accordance with the Fund's policies on such consultations, and will provide the Fund with such information as the Fund may request in connection with the implementation of the policies of the program.

5. The Fund will conduct with the Government of Chad a review of progress in the implementation of the program supported by the first annual ESAF arrangement, to be completed no later than March 15, 1996.
Very truly yours,
Mahamat Ahmat Alhabo Minister of Finance and Computer Services
Mariam Mahamat Nour Minister of Planning and Cooperation President of the High Interministerial Committee
Attachment: Memorandum on Economic and Financial Policies for 1995-96
CHAD
Memorandum on Economic and_Financial Policies
N'Djaména, August 8, 1995
1. Introduction

1. The political and economic fundamentals in Chad are undergoing profound changes. On the political side, a process toward pluralistic government was set in motion with the convening of a sovereign national conference in early 1993. During the extensive deliberations that followed, agreement was reached on the outline of a new constitution, the formation of a transition Government and High Transition Council, and the establishment of an electoral calendar for the first democratic presidential and parliamentary elections in early l996. On the economic side, the devaluation of the CFA franc in January l994 has provided a strong stimulus to economic activity, and the related resumption of a close cooperation with the Fund and the Bank has encouraged the formulation of a coherent economic policy program that was supported by a stand-by arrangement (SBA) from the Fund, and an Economic Recovery Credit (ERC) from the World Bank. The objectives and aims of economic policy are no less ambitious than those for the political system. They rest on the assumption that the strengthening of individual decision making and private initiative will foster both political stability and improved economic performance.

2. The authorities are pursuing the political and economic reforms in a difficult environment. Years of civil wars and strife, and a successive number of adverse external developments, including a persistent deterioration in the terms of trade, resulted in a serious deterioration in the economic, social, and administrative infrastructure. They have also undermined the emergence of a degree of stability that is essential for investment and economic activity. This poses a challenge that the authorities have to face in a situation of tight finances but also in a more promising economic environment due to the devaluation of the CFA franc. However, there is no shortage of will, toughness, energy, and resilience in the country. To fully harness these inherent qualities of the Chadian people, it is the authorities' firm intention to pursue appropriate policies for economic stabilization, and to broader and deepen structural reform. In support of this decision, the authorities are requesting from the Fund a three-year arrangement covering the period July l, l995 to June 30, 1998 under the enhanced structural adjustment facility (ESAF) for the equivalent of SDR 49.56 million (l20 percent of quota). The authorities hope that their economic and structural adjustment program for this period will be supported by sufficient financial assistance from partner countries, the World Bank, and other multilateral institutions.

3. The devaluation of the CFA franc in January $1994$ offered Chad a unique opportunity to break out of the vicious circle of disintegrating public administration and declining private sector activity that had gripped the country in recent years. It also gave the authorities a chance to build confidence in their economic policies in the context of a coherent economic program. Performance under the program, which, from March l994, was supported by a stand-by arrangement from the Fund, was initially mixed. On the one hand, the devaluation resulted in the needed correction of relative prices and incentives to economic agents, and spurred a stronger output and export response than was projected. On the other hand, the implementation of the economic program for l994 suffered initially from a lack of broad consensus for, and ownership of, the program, severe weaknesses in the definition and allocation of administrative responsibilities, and a growing demotivation of public administration stemming from a large accumulation of salary arrears. The related slippages in performance were aggravated by adverse external factors, with the result that the program veered off track soon after it was launched.

4.The failure to meet the performance criteria for June $1994$ resulted in a critical and constructive examination of its causes and a wide range of remedial actions, which demonstrated the authorities' resolve to take charge of the many exigencies of program implementation. First, to remedy the problems of program ownership and policy responsibilities, they created a system of committees for the "preparation, negotiation, and monitoring of structural adjustment programs." Second, to demonstrate their commitment to economic reform, they implemented from October l994 a staff-monitored program, which involved a considerable strengthening of structural policies relative to the program supported by the SBA and attempted to make up in part for the ground loss in the first half of 1994. Third, to address the weaknesses observed in program implementation and in the administration, they introduced a number of changes in the organization and staffing of key ministries, including notably the Ministry of Finance and its revenue departments. Finally, to strengthen the operations of the critical customs department, where problems had been particularly acute, they sought technical assistance in customs policies, procedures, and operations.

5.  The results of the decisive strengthening of policies and program implementation since July l994 have been clear and encouraging. The weaknesses of revenue collection have been largely resolved, with the result that average monthly tax revenue rose from CFAF 1.6 billion during the first half of l994 to CFAF 4.4 billion during the last quarter of $1994$ ."In the event, almost all quantitative benchmarks of the staff-monitored program were met at end-December l994, with the exception of those applicable to external debt service obligations and the resumption of regular payments of salaries to the civil service, which continued to suffer from the initial weaknesses in domestic revenue mobilization and a large shortfall in external financial assistance. Moreover, the authorities made great efforts to implement the structural policy measures that constituted notional benchmarks under the monitored program, with the result that virtually all such

benchmarks were observed at end-December. The exception was that of the benchmark on the collection of tax arrears from the 20 largest firms, where disputes over amounts and legal procedures, as well as complexities in settling cross-debts, slowed implementation. A similarly positive result has been observed with respect to the March 1995 benchmarks. In particular, revenue collection continued to improve so that the benchmark for tax revenue was surpassed by more than CFAF 1 billion. This allowed the resumption of more regular wage payments in l995 without, however, allowing for a net reduction in the stock of salary arrears. At the same time, there was a moderate further accumulation of external payments arrears, including some CFAF 1.4 billion that were eligible for Paris Club rescheduling. All monetary and external loan benchmarks for March l995 were observed.

6.  While the recent improvements in economic performance and program implementation are appreciable, they are no more than a start. The authorities realize that there remain important obstacles to a lasting improvement in economic performance and that persistent efforts are needed to raise the standard of living in the country to a satisfactory level. To move in this direction, and with a view to stabilizing expectations and strengthening accountability, they are determined to set clear and unmistakable signals for their medium-term economic strategy. Moreover, to strengthen commitment and credibility from the outset, they are proposing a demanding agenda of economic policy measures for the first year of their medium-term adjustment program.
III. Medium-Term Strategy

7.  The authorities' medium-term strategy will pursue macroeconomic stability and structural reform in parallel, strengthen and broaden public infrastructure in support of private sector development, and improve the functioning of public administration. They will also give particular attention to issues of absorptive capacity, debt sustainability, and concessionality of financing. In particular, they will continue to pursue prudent fiscal policies aimed at the achievement of fiscal and external viability before the end of the decade irrespective of the prospect of large revenues from the exploitation of oil reserves in the not-too-distant future. At the same time, they will make appropriate preparations for a smooth and effective integration of the oil sector into the economy. Regarding developments in other areas, the authorities intend to give priority to agriculture and livestock, the two sectors in which Chad has a comparative advantage, before considering diversification policies in favor of other sectors.

8.  Stabilization policies and medium-term structural reforms for the program period aim at achieving a satisfactory growth rate, low inflation, and a viable external balance (a more detailed and numerical description of the program objectives is contained in the accompanying policy framework paper). Progress in this direction will build on fiscal policies that
increase public sector saving, and a set of measures that strengthen Chad's external competitiveness, raise confidence and increase private saving and investment.

9. The focus of structural reform is the revitalization of the private sector and the more general strengthening of the response mechanisms of the economy. The authorities will, therefore, accelerate their public enterprise divestiture program. They will also continue their policies of price and trade liberalization and sharpen competition through the removal of the few remaining monopolies, as well as by easing access to markets and facilitating the creation of enterprises. During the program period, the authorities will also seek to simplify legislation and regulation affecting private sector operations and stabilize firms' legal environment so as to ease recourse to judicial redress when necessary. In this context, they will adapt the labor code to strengthen the contractual rights of social partners, adjust the investment code to current proposals for the region, and introduce a comprehensive commercial code.

10. Reforms in support of private sector development will be complemented by energetic action on the functioning and efficiency of public administration, and a rationalization of budgeting choices. A principal element in this context will be the initiation and execution of a civil service reform, which will seek to adjust employee composition and skills in public administration to the new priorities and objectives under a pluralistic government. It will also seek to increase efficiency through training, a clearer definition of career streams, and a strengthening of incentives, including through a remuneration system that adequately rewards performance. Special attention will be given to the effective design and execution of economic and structural policy. To this end, the authorities will continue to make extensive use of the current system of committees and improve and broaden its functions where necessary, in particular in the area of collection and dissemination of vital statistics. Finally, with assistance of the donor community, the authorities intend to review and identify measures needed to upgrade budget preparation, execution, and control. The objectives of the study will include the identification of measures to increase the transparency of budget operations, achieve the complete elimination of off-budget operations, and strengthen the financial management of the national retirement and social security funds (CNRT and CNPS, respectively).

11. The central element of financial policies during the program period will be a durable strengthening of the budgetary position. To this end, the authorities intend to shift the current primary balance of the Central Government from a deficit of 6 percent of GDP during the period l993-94 to a surplus of about 1 percent in l998. Action to that effect will concentrate on a substantial improvement in revenue performance by way of a further strengthening of tax administration and collection, a broadening of the tax base, and a simplification of tax legislation, regulation, and procedures. On the expenditure side, a strict prioritization will be necessary throughout the program period. Needed increases in spending on health, education, and financial services will therefore have to be compensated in good measure by economies in nonpriority areas. Moreover, the government wage bill will have to remain under tight control, and the scope for a curtailment of transfers and subsidies to be exploited in full.

12. Monetary policy will continue to be formulated and conducted by the BEAC at the regional level, and will assure compatibility of financial developments with the external and inflation objectives of the program. It will also seek to assist the intended retrenchment of the public sector and expansion of the private sector. To strengthen financial intermediation and the efficiency of financial sector operations, the authorities will work at the level of the BEAC so that monetary management increasingly rely on market-based instruments of control, including market-determined interest rates. Moreover, to strengthen confidence in the financial sector, the authorities will lend their full support to the regional banking commission (COBAC) in effectively enforcing prudential regulations. Consideration will also be given to ways and means of broadening financial intermediation and expanding financial sector activities to the rural areas.
IV. The Program for the Period July l995-June 1996

13. The first program year under the requested ESAF will cover the period July l995-June l996, and is set up as a seamless continuation of the staffmonitored program for the period October 1994-June 1995. The macroeconomic objectives for the two calendar years spanned by the first program are predicated on policies that would allow (i) real GDP to grow by around 5 1/2 percent in 1995, and 5 1/4 percent in 1996, reflecting in particular the strong recovery of the cotton sector; (ii) inflation (as measured by the consumer price index), which flared up strongly in l994, to decline to an average rate of 6 1/2 percent in l995, followed by a further reduction to 4 1/2 percent in l996; and (iii) the external current account deficit (before official transfers) to decline moderately from 22.7 percent of GDP in 1994 to about 2l percent in 1995/96, as the growth of exports is largely offset by increases in public investment and a further strengthening of private sector import demand as a result of increases in incomes and the replenishment of stocks.
1. Fiscal policy

14. The authorities recognize that, given Chad's exchange arrangements, the rehabilitation of the public finances is central to the restoration of macroeconomic balance and the consolidation of confidence. It is therefore their intent to reduce the current primary deficit of the public finances from around 6.0 percent of GDP in l994 to 2.2 percent in l995, and further to l.3 percent in l996. Given the pressing spending needs in a number of areas, a significant improvement in revenue performance is critical for the restoration of sound public finances and a normalization of the functioning of public administration. It is therefore the authorities' objective to increase the share of domestic revenue in GDP from its exceptionally low level of 7.4 percent in 1994 to 9 percent in l995, and close to 10 percent: in l996. Prospects for achieving the revenue objectives have improved

considerably over the past couple of months as a result of the introduction of a number of important policy measures. First, as noted in paragraph 4 above, significant efforts have been made to improve the functioning of revenue departments, including through the replacement of nonperforming officials. Second, decisive action has been taken to eliminate fraud, unwarranted tax and customs duty exemptions, and outside interference at customs. Finally, a comprehensive mechanism to assess and collect back taxes has been started and has begun to show results.

15. The authorities intend to expand their efforts in these areas and strengthen them as needed. In particular, they will take all requisite measures to achieve an efficient implementation of the reform measures called for by the UDEAC tax and customs tariff reform. Effective June l, 1995,they have replaced a set of five separate indirect taxes by a single turnover tax with only two rates, with the results that the tax base has been considerably broadened and that assessment and procedures have been greatly simplified. Moreover, with effect from the same date, excise taxes have been introduced on a certain number of goods, including alcohol, tobacco, and other luxury goods. To further strengthen revenue performance in l996, the authorities will renegotiate, before the end of l995, the remaining special conventions (conventions d'établissement), so as to eliminate full or partial tax and customs duty exemptions to three enterprises (COTONTCHAD, MCT, SONASUT). By the same date, they will also implement a reform of other special tax and customs regimes (including those allowed by the Investment Code) in line with UDEAC agreements. Moreover, to further improve tax administration and collection, broaden the tax base, and strengthen coordination between revenue departments, they will make effective, at the latest by end-l995, a single, computerized taxpayer code and file. A decree to this effect has been issued in July l995. Furthermore, the authorities will begin preparations for a significant simplification of direct taxes and increase the practice of withholding at source with a view to increasing revenue from direct taxes. A study on the simplification of direct taxes will be undertaken with the assistance of the Fund before the end of June l996. Finally, the authorities will take steps for the timely introduction of a value added tax and of taxation of the informal sector in the form of a business license system (patente).

16. Particular attention will continue to be given to the operations and functioning of the customs department. A significant improvement in the operations of the department has already been achieved as a result of staffing changes, the elimination of rampant interference by paramilitary forces, organizational reforms, and technical assistance on both general customs issues and the collection of import duties on petroleum products. The authorities recognize that more is needed. Consequently, a detailed action plan has been worked out in collaboration with the Fund's Fiscal Affairs Department (FAD) and a resident FAD expert. This plan will continue to be implemented in accordance with a priority list of actions. The authorities hope that the timely and successful implementation of the plan will benefit from donor assistance in the planned upgrading of equipment in customs offices and the relocation of the principal customs office to the main crossing point from Cameroon to Chad. Among the other priority

measures, the creation of information units will permit automated monitoring of and control over customs operations held in suspense, and the updating at regular intervals of the system of reference values, which will allow for the assessment of customs duties on a more appropriate basis. In this context, any remaining use of standard values for imports will as soon as possible be replaced by the application of assessed CIF values. Furthermore, to curtail abuse in the granting of exemptions for diplomatic imports, reference quotas for these imports will be established in collaboration with embassies. A similar monitoring and control mechanism will be considered for the exemption enjoyed by nongovernmental organizations (NGOs), projectrelated imports, and public procurements. Finally, to further reduce tax evasion and fraud, and advance the settlement of payments due, the authorities will strengthen the effectiveness of customs controls and impose tight restrictions on procedures in suspense for customs duty payments.

17. On the expenditure side, the authorities will be guided by the need, or the one hand, to restore credibility through a full respect of the budget, and to reorder budget priorities on the other. Among these priorities, the expansion of the health and education sectors constitutes the principal element of social policy. For a better rationalization of public expenditure decisions, and to achieve a structural improvement in expenditure management, the authorities will conduct a comprehensive public expenditure review by end-September l995, in collaboration with the World Bank. The authorities also intend to improve the monitoring and currentness of their payments obligations. In this context, they will establish a complete inventory of payments arrears to civil servants and domestic suppliers. To increase efficiency in public administration and reduce its costs, the authorities will also strive for civil service reform and will undertake, before the end of the first program year, a comprehensive study of how best to proceed. The study will examine in particular the modalities of a replacement of the system of automatic step increases by a promotion system based on merit.

18. Regarding wages and salaries, the authorities face the dual task of keeping the wage bill under control in an environment of limited resources, and of achieving regularity of wage paymerts at an adequate level so as to maintain appropriate incentives to work and foster social peace. As a structural measure, a comprehensive civil service census will be conducted by end-November l995 to improve the control and management of the wage bill. Moreover, effective January l, l996, a single, harmonized and computerized payroll file will be established. For l995, the objective is to hold the wage bill to a maximum of CFAF 27.4 billion on a cash basis (including payments already made on salary arrears carried over from previous budget years), implying a growth in nominal terms roughly equal to the rate of inflation. Adherence to this objective will require a substantial effort, in particular because of two factors that have increased the trend rate of growth of the wage bill. First, in line with agreements reached under the social pact, nominal wages and salaries have been increased by l0 percent under the l995 budget. Second, employment in the priority areas of health, education, and financial services has been increased by some 500 people. Tc compensate for these measures, retirement rules will be strictly enforced

and ghost employment will be eliminated. Together, these measures should permit to fully offset the increase in employment in the priority sectors. Moreover, selected discretionary measures such as reductions in family allowances, in diplomatic personnel, and in travel allowances will be taken. Maintaining at the same time regularity and equity of payments of wages and salaries will require that inequalities in current payments between ministries and government agencies be eliminated in l995. For 1996, the objective is to keep the wage bill constant in real terms, implying a growth in nominal terms of no more than 4.5 percent. To meet this objective, while continuing the employment program for priority sectors, increases in wage and salary rates will again have to be very modest and further reductions in staff in nonpriority areas will be necessary. At the same time, however, there is likely to be some room for the repayment of salary arrears, which will be used in the first instance for repayments to those employees who have the "oldest" arrears.

19. Among other expenditure, military outlays stand out. To reduce spending in this area on a lasting basis, the authorities will initially aim for a further reduction in military personnel by 4,O0o men by the end of 1995,with the help of financing already secured for that purpose. If needed, new legislative measures will be introduced to reach this objective. To reach the target of a 25,000-strong army by end-June l996, down from some 32,000 men at present, the authorities will seek additional external support and financing for demobilization expenses. Regarding government transfers. and subsidies, an inventory and action plan will be established by end-1995, and implemented from l996 onwards to reduce progressively their weight in the budget. The need for subsidization has already been reduced by increases in administered prices in the state-owned sugar and utility companies. A further l0 percent increase in electricity tariffs has become effective on July l, l995. Additional increases in utility prices and prices of other public enterprises will be decided as needed in the context of performance contracts. Against the economies to be realized in these areas, spending will have to be increased selectively elsewhere. In particular, reflecting the priority accorded to health and education, outlays on goods and services in these sectors will be increased by the equivalent of 20 percent in real terms in both l995 and l996. Before endDecember l995, the authorities will also allocate out of budgetary resources for l995 the equivalent of CFAF 1.2 billion to the purchase of essential generic drugs, as part of social safety net measures. Moreover, to create employment--especially in the main urban areas, where the problem of unemployment is most acute--spending will be raised on three labor-intensive public infrastructure programs (ATETIP, PADS, TSP II) in line with available external financing.
2. Monetary and financial sector policy

20. Within the framework of the regional monetary arrangements, the authorities intend to implement a prudent credit and interest rate policy, consistent with the balance of payments objectives of the program and the need to forestall adverse inflation expectations. For the pursuit of these policies, they will advocate in the relevant regional bodies an increasing reliance on indirect instruments of monetary policy. In particular, they will advocate that the current auction system of central bank credit be complemented by the effective introduction of reverse auctions that would permit the control of bank credit by way of sales of BEAC or BEAC-backed securities. Moreover, they will seek to have (l) the current auction system, under which both price and quantity are controlled, amended so as to let interest rates clear the market; and (2) the rediscountability of the so-called "productive investment" credits under the B-window (Guichet B) brought under the refinancing limits, and ultimately rescinded. Furthermore, the liberalization of interest rates will be encouraged by the elimination of the minimum deposit rate (with the exception of the rate on small passbook savings), and by the substitution of a usury rate for the maximum lending rate, which might also be done away with altogether. At the same time, in concert with the regional bank supervisory body (CoBAC), the authorities will insist on rigorous adherence to the prudential ratios established for the BEAc region. In this context, they will conclude the liquidation of the BICIT bank before end-September l995. Regarding the financing of economic development, the authorities remain concerned about the low level of credit extended by banks to the private sector despite the recent elimination of the credit tax. They also believe that the availability of medium- and long-term credit will need to be increased to foster a dynamic development of private sector activity. With interested donors, they will therefore commission a study on these specific issues, as well as on the more general question of broadening and deepening financial intermediation in Chad.
3. Price policies

21. The authorities are committed to price liberalization and will continue their reforms with the removal of the few remaining price controls. Effective July 1, 1995, price controls on sugar and the setting of profit margins for wholesalers and retailers have been eliminated. Similarly, the ordinance on price controls on edible oils and soap, both of which are by-products of cotton ginning, were lifted at end-June l995, and the relevant clause on the setting of prices in the convention with COTONTCHAD will be removed before end-l995. In the pharmaceutical sector, fixed profit margins for pharmaceutical products will be abandoned to stimulate competition. However, for social reasons, the prices of the 60 most important generic drugs will remain under government control for the time being. Prices of petroleum products will continue to move in line with world market prices. In the cotton sector, the guaranteed producer price for first grade cotton has been set at CFAF l40 per kilo for the l995/96 season. At the end of the season, the guaranteed price will be supplemented by a premium to farmers that will be determined once the financial results of the COTONTCHAD are known. Before end-l995, the Government will also seek to reach agreement with the other shareholders of COToNTCHAD about the repayment of the Government's debt toward COTONTCHAD (which reflects cumulative losses of COTONTCHAD as a result of the setting of too high producer prices), and the treatment of service payments on loans by the French CFD that were onlent to COTONTCHAD by the Government, but were canceled by the CFD in 1994.

22. Divestiture of public enterprises will be accelerated during the first program year. Before end-l995, auctions will be launched for a number of public or semi-public enterprises (listed in the policy framework paper) in line with agreements reached with the World Bank. Moreover, procedures wil: be sped up for the liquidation of the Petroleum Fund (Fonds Pétrolier) and the Sugar Fund (Caisse Sucre) before end-l995. In addition, studies on a disengagement strategy will be initiated for the National Post and Telecommunications Office (ONPT), and the International Telecommunications Company (TIT). In the banking sector, the authorities will seek to complete the privatization of the Development Bank (BDT) and to find a solution for the BMBT (whose principal shareholder, the Meridien BIAO SA (Luxembourg), holding 74 percent of the share capital, is under liquidation procedures). Regarding the public utility (STEE), the authorities will continue to seek cost savings and increases in productivity; corrective measures will concentrate on raising efficiency in billing procedures and bill collection. In addition, the authorities will henceforth include in the national budget the full amount of the anticipated costs of government electricity and water consumption. They will also definitely settle the cross-debts between the Government and the STEE, including by way of compensation operations. In the cotton sector, an action plan for improved performance will be established and implemented on the basis of a technical audit of COTONTCHAD. In the sugar sector, more flexible sugar import procedures will be adopted in order to improve market supply and strengthen competition in the sugar market; this policy would also constitute an element of fraud control. Moreover, the tax and customs treatment of the SONASUT will be brought in line with the general UDEAC regime and the temporary surtax on sugar removed. Finally, a study on the different options for a possible divestiture of the Government from the SONASUT will be carried out before end-June 1996.

23. As part of the regulatory reform to foster private sector development, the authorities will expedite the elaboration of a set of business regulations that will place the operations of enterprises on a surer footing. The investment code will also be revised in line with regional integration objectives. Finally, to reduce rigidities in the labor market and encourage employment creation, the authorities will undertake a comprehensive revision of the Labor Code. In particular, Articles ll6 and 142 of the Code regarding wage setting and minimum wages will be amended before the end of 1995, so as to give social partners (employers and employees, and their representatives) the right to negotiate freely amongst themselves wage rates, including minimum wages. At the same time, the authorities will seek to foster harmonious social relations; and encourage the social partners to strengthen their consultation mechanisms at the sectoral and/or enterprise level.

24. Although Chad still avails itself of the transitional arrangements of Article XIV of the Fund's Articles of Agreement, its exchange regulations are already fully compatible with the obligations under Article VIlI status. During the program period, the authorities do not intend to introduce or intensify any exchange restrictions, introduce or modify any multiple currency practices, conclude any bilateral payments agreements that are inconsistent with Article VIII of the IMF's Articles of Agreement, or introduce or intensify import restrictions for balance of payments reasons. Moreover, in concert with the other member countries of the BEAC, Chad plans to accept the obligations of Article VIII, Sections 2, 3, and 4 in due course. Limits on travel allowances will be administered in a nonrestrictive manner, and all bona fide requests in excess of these limits will be granted without undue delay. Before end-l995, they will update Chad's exchange regulations and bring them in line with the text endorsed by the BEAC Ministerial Committee in September l993. Moreover, before end-l995, the authorities will remove the system of import and export licensing by repealing the decree determining the modalities for imports, allotment, circulation, and distribution of goods (Decree No. 282/PR/MCI/89 of 1989), as well as the decree regulating imports and exports (Decree No. 1l3/ET of 1966). They will establish a negative list of products import of which will be subject to special authorization for reasons of public health, public security, and environmental protection. The authorities will also ensure that needed documentation on imports and exports will be communicated to the revenue departments and the external financing departments of the Ministry of Finance, as well as the Central Bank (BEAC), in order to permit an appropriate monitoring of current international transactions, notably with respect to purchases of foreign currency and for the establishment of reliable balance of payments data. Since January l, 1995, all export taxes have been eliminated. In addition, effective January l, l996, the authorities will reduce the intra-UDEAC import duty rate from the current 20 percent of the common external tariff to 10 percent. To assure a timely servicing of Chad's external debt, the authorities have requested technical assistance from the World Bank in the computerization and management of public sector debt and debt service obligations. Before end-l995, they will present an action plan on the strengthening of debt and debt service management. They expect to eliminate all arrears on external debt service before the end of l995, but, in the case of delays in external financing, latest before the end of the first program year--in part through a further Paris Club rescheduling--and to remain current on their payments obligations thereafter.

25. The ESAF arrangement is expected to play a catalytic role in the mobilization of bilateral and multilateral financial assistance to Chad. Chad's external financing requirements during the period of the first annual ESAF arrangement are expected to be covered on the basis of disbursements under aid commitments already made (CFAF 28.8 billion in l995 and CFAF l6.l billion in l996), as well as by a new terms of reference Paris Club rescheduling of eligible debt service (CFAF 0.5 billion in 1995 and CFAF 1.1 billion in l996), and a deferral of debt service on the postal debt (CFAF 3.6 billion in l995). Under these assumptions, the residual financing gaps of CFAF 32.9 billion in l995 and CFAF 17.2 billion in l996 will be closed. A modest financing gap is envisaged for the period l997-98, for which donors have not yet drawn up definitive aid plans. A successful implementation of the program should also permit additional debt relief.
VI. Program Monitoring

26. Given the importance attributed in the program to revenue performance, the following actions have already been taken: (a) effective implementation of the new turnover tax, excise taxes, and customs duty rates consistent with the UDEAC reform; (b) publication of a decree providing for a single taxpayer code and file; and (c) assignment of exclusive responsibility of the customs administration for assessment and collection of taxes on international trade. In addition to these revenue measures, the following measures have been taken: (a) abolition of the decrees fixing the prices of edible oils and soap; (b) increases in electricity tariffs by l0 percent; and (c) elimination of controls on sugar prices, and of the prescription of profit margins for wholesalers and retailers.

27. Performance under the first-year ESAF arrangement will be monitored on the basis of the following financial performance criteria (end-December 1995): (a) net domestic assets of the Central Bank (BEAC); (b) net credit of the banking system to the Government; (c) the minimum collection of domestic tax revenue; (d) the wage bill of the central government; (e) minimum reductions in domestic payments arrears; l/ (f) the elimination of external payments arrears, and an injunction against the accumulation of new external arrears; and (g) a prohibition on the contracting and guaranteeing of loans on nonconcessional terms, with maturities in the range of l-15 years. These performance criteria will be supplemented by an indicator on changes in the net domestic assets of the banking system. Structural performance criteria include (a) the elimination, before end-l995, of import and export licenses; and (b) the amendment, before end-l995, of Articles ll6 and l42 of the Labor Code to give employers and employees the right to freely determine wage rates, including minimum wages. Structural benchmarks include (a) the reduction il the preferential intra-UDEAC tariff (TPG) from 20 percent of common UDEAC external tariff (TEC) to 10 percent, effective January l, l996; (b) the renegotiation, before end-l995, of special tax and customs treatment (enterprise conventions) for private companies and public enterprises

(COTONTCHAD, MCT, and SONASUT), and of the Investment Code, to bring them in line with the common UDEAC regime; (c) the promulgation, before end-l995, of exchange regulations in line with the text adopted for the BEAC zone in September l993; (d) the establishment, before end-l995, of an action program to improve performance of COTONTCHAD; (e) presentation of a study on alternative options for the privatization of SONASUT, by end-June 1996; (f) presentation of a study, with the assistance of the Fund, on the simplification of direct taxes, by end-June l996; and (g) presentation of a study, with the assistance of the Fund, on possible modalities and timing of the introduction of a value-added tax, by end-June, 1996.

28. Quantitative benchmarks will apply to end-September 1995 and quantitative and structural performance criteria to end-December l995. The second disbursement under the first annual arrangement will be conditional upon observance of the quantitative and structural performance criteria at end-December l995 and completion of the midterm review, which will take place no later than March 15, 1996. All data necessary for monitoring performance under the program will be collected and made available on a timely basis. The review will reassess the indicative benchmarks set for end-March and end-June l996 and pay particular attention to the progress being made in revenue collection, currentness of external debt service payments, and on the reduction in payments arrears to suppliers.

text: VII. Exchange Rate Arrangement

Chad is a member of the Central Bank of the Central African States (BEAC). The exchange system, common to all members of the BEAC, has been free of restrictions on payments and transfers for current international transactions. Repurchase of the CFA franc bank notes exported outside the BEAC was suspended on August 2, l993. The BEAC common currency is the CFA franc, which is pegged to the French franc. Effective January l2, 1994, the CFA franc was devalued by 50 percent in foreign currency terms, and the exchange rate was adjusted from F ${\bf\dot{\mathrm{~\bf~1~}~}}={\bf C}{\bf F}{\bf A}{\bf F}$ 50 to F l = CFAF l00. On December 30, $1994$ , the rate of the CFA franc, in terms of the SDR, was CFAF $780.44\ =\ 5\tt{D R}\ 1$ .
VIII. Article IV Consultations

(a) Chad is on the standard l2-month consultation cycle. (b) Discussions for the l994 Article IV consultation were held in N'Djaména during the period November 5-20, 1994. The staff report (SM/95/21) was discussed by the Executive Board on February 17, 1995 (EBM/95/l7). A background paper (SM/95/3l) was also circulated to the Board.
Ix. Technical Assistance

1988: FAD mission on the preparation of a tax reform program.   
1989: FAD mission on the preparation of a tax reform program.   
1990: FAD mission for reviewing implementation of the tax reform program.   
1991: FAD mission for reviewing implementation of the tax reform program.   
1994: FAD mission for assessing weaknesses in customs administration and recruitment of an expert responsible for strengthening customs administration.
X. Resident Representative None.

At the beginning of the Bank's support of structural adjustment in Chad, a Financial Rehabilitation Credit (FRC) was approved in July 1988. The objectives of the program were to re-establish the financial profitability of the cotton sector and to strengthen policy reforms in public finances. However, owing to the deteriorating fiscal performance, the FRC was suspended in July l99l. The residual balance was canceled in December 1992 and the credit was closed.

The Bank's strategy for Chad supports investments in the productive sectors (agriculture, livestock, and energy) as well as transport infrastructure. In 1994, particular emphasis has been given to the support of the devaluation with a $\mathtt{U S S20}$ million Economic Recovery Credit (ERC) and the development of human resources and the strengthening of economic management (GEF).

text: B. Statement of IFC Investments
Chad is not a member of IFC.
C. Activities Under Preparation

In l995, the World Bank will continue and expand its technical assistance program (Economic and Financial Management Project-GEF). Preparations are also under way for the eventual negotiation of a structural adjustment credit (SAC) that would accompany an ESAF-supported program of the Fund. Relevant Bank-related issues for the related policy framework paper and policy matrix are being examined). Moreover, a public expenditure review is to be concluded by September l995. Finally, support to the Chad/Cameroon Petroleum Pipeline Project is under consideration.
